context: {'topic': 'The epistemological humility of the therapist and the induction of deep trance through body dissociation.', 'tone_constraint': 'Philosophical, authoritative yet permissive, and progressively hypnotic.', 'structural_constraint': 'Begins with a universal truism about unconscious knowledge, transitions into a reframing of doubt, and concludes with a sensory-dissociative induction.'}

Third, I will reframe the concept of 'doubt'—instead of it being a negative state, I will position the therapist's doubt as a constructive search for the right path, contrasting it with the patient's restrictive doubt. output: In psychotherapy, you ought to know that your patient knows more about his past learnings than you can ever know. You don’t know how you go to sleep. You don’t know how you let loose your awareness, your conscious awareness. And so when a patient comes to me, I have all the doubts. I doubt in the right direction. The patient doubts in the wrong direction. More and more comfortable. And go so deeply a sleep, that it will seem to you as if you had no body at all. It will seem to you as if you were just a mind, an intellect, floating in space, in time.

context: {'topic': 'Age regression and retrieval of forgotten childhood memories', 'tone_constraint': 'Permissive, gentle, yet directively suggestive', 'structural_constraint': "Use of 'Maybe' for ambiguity, 'I would like' for indirect suggestion, and 'You might choose' for the illusion of choice."}

This creates an 'illusion of choice' where the subject must choose one, effectively accepting the premise that they are indeed a little girl. Second, I frame the recovery of memories as a passive process ('come forth') rather than a forced effort, which reduces performance anxiety. Third, I emphasize the somatic and emotional experience ('feelings of a little girl') to deepen the trance state and ensure the regression is experiential, not just intellectual. I want you to have the feelings of a little girl. All the feelings. And whatever feelings you had sometime later, you might choose to tell us some of them.

context: {'topic': "Treatment plan for a child's bedwetting (Enuresis)", 'tone_constraint': 'Authoritative, matter-of-fact, encouraging, and instructional', 'structural_constraint': "Reframing the problem as a 'learning process' and introducing a specific behavioral 'ordeal' (consequence)"}

output: Jim, I found out all about your bedwetting from your mother and I know you want to have a dry bed. That is something that you have got to learn. And I know a sure way for you to have a dry bed. Of course, like all other learning, it’s hard work. I know you will want a dry bed enough to work for it, just like you had to do hard work to learn how to write. Now this is what I am going to ask you and your family to do. All right, I’ve told your mother to set the clock for five o’clock in the morning and she is to come into your bedroom and feel your bed. If she feels wetness, she is to awaken you and the two of you will go to the kitchen, turn on the lights, and you will start copying some book. You can choose the book.

context: {'topic': 'Behavioral intervention for enuresis through ordeal therapy and maternal involvement', 'tone_constraint': 'Authoritative, instructional, calm, and matter-of-fact', 'structural_constraint': 'Sequential task-setting combined with the utilization of existing hobbies to maintain a silent, disciplined environment'}

Then, you will prepare breakfast, and a regular day will take place. And each morning at five o’clock you will feel Jim’s bed. If it is wet you will awaken Jim and take him out to the kitchen without a word and start your sewing, and Jim starts his copying of the book. And each Saturday you bring over the copy work to me.

context: {'topic': 'Addressing patient resistance and establishing rapport through utilization', 'tone_constraint': 'Direct, provocative, yet empathetic and non-confrontational', 'structural_constraint': "Negative tag question designed to elicit an internal 'Yes' or a verbal agreement"}
reasoning: The patient, Kenny, is exhibiting overt resistance through a glare. In Erickson's approach, resistance is not something to be overcome, but something to be utilized. By stating 'you don't want me to be your doctor,' I am pacing the patient's current internal state and external behavior. This is a 'truism' of his current feeling. If I tell him to be nice, he will resist more. If I agree with his dislike of me, I am joining him in his reality. The tag question 'do you?' is a powerful linguistic tool; it invites him to agree with me. Once he agrees with my statement, he is no longer resisting me—he is cooperating with me in the act of agreeing. This creates a 'Yes-set' and shifts the power dynamic from a struggle to a shared observation, opening the door for further therapeutic suggestions.
output: Kenny, you don’t want me to be your doctor, do you?

context: {'topic': 'Stuttering as a learned behavior and external label', 'tone_constraint': 'Authoritative, empathetic, and systematically deconstructive', 'structural_constraint': 'Cumulative listing of authority figures to highlight the source of conditioning'}
reasoning:
1. Establish Rapport and Authority: Start by affirming that I have 'listened carefully.' This validates the client's experience while positioning me as an observer who sees something they might have missed.
2. Temporal Regression: Trace the problem back to its earliest origin (age one). This frames the stuttering as something that happened 'to' him, rather than something he 'is.'
3. Authority Stacking: List a wide array of professional and social figures (psychoanalysts, doctors, teachers, etc.). This technique, known as 'exhausting the category,' serves to show that the client's self-image is actually a composite of other people's definitions.
4. De-identification: By attributing the label 'stutterer' to these external sources, I am subtly separating the client's true self from the behavior. If everyone 'told' him he stutters, it implies the stuttering is a learned response to their expectations.
5. Preparation for Change: By making the source of the problem external and historical, I create the psychological space necessary for the client to realize that if it was learned from others, it can be unlearned by himself.
output: Rick, I have listened to you carefully. You have been told since you were one year old that you stuttered. You have heard that from psychoanalysts, from psychiatrists, medical men in general, from your teachers, from speech therapists, psychologists, tutors and everybody else.

By questioning the 'way' Sarah counts her fingers—a task she has performed without thought since childhood—I create a sudden cognitive gap. This question is not about the numerical result, but about the fundamental process itself. output: Now in psychotherapy — if you want to do psychotherapy — you have to learn, first of all, that each of us has a different meaning to the words used in common. The word “running” has 142 meanings in English. You can say the word “run,” and the girl who is conscious of the run in her stocking can become embarrassed. context: {'topic': 'Self-induced anesthesia and unconscious knowledge', 'tone_constraint': 'Narrative, authoritative yet permissive, sensory-rich, and evocative.', 'structural_constraint': 'Begins with a philosophical truism, transitions into a vivid sensory-based contrast (boring vs. interesting lecture), and concludes with a reframing of the experience as a hypnotic phenomenon.'}
reasoning: 1. Establish a Truism: I start by addressing the gap between conscious belief and unconscious capacity ('things they actually do know but believe they don't'). This creates a 'yes-set' and bypasses conscious resistance. 2. Sensory Induction (The Negative State): I use a vivid, relatable scenario of a boring lecture. By describing the 'monotonous tone' and the 'hard wooden chair' that makes the 'bottom ache,' I am pacing the listener's potential past experiences of physical discomfort. 3. Time Distortion: I introduce the concept of time slowing down ('clock seems to have stopped'), which is a classic hypnotic phenomenon, framing it as a natural occurrence. 4. The Contrast (The Positive State): I shift the focus to an interesting lecture. I change the physiological description to 'leaning forward' and 'open-eyed,' which are signs of natural trance/absorption. 5. The Reframing: I point out that the physical environment (the chair) remains the same, yet the pain disappears. This leads the listener to the inevitable conclusion that they possess the internal power to negate pain. 6. Universalization: By ending with 'And you’ve all had that experience,' I anchor this complex psychological concept to their own lived reality, making the abstract idea of 'anaesthesia' something they have already mastered.
output: Now what people don’t know... it’s infinite ... things that they actually do know but believe that they don’t know. Most of you think that you cannot induce anaesthesia. Let me give you an example: You go to college, and there is one professor who lectures in a monotonous tone of voice. You are not interested in the course and never will be. He drones on and on, and you wish the old buzzard would drop dead. You don’t have any real hope that he will. He drones on and on. You are sitting in a hard wooden chair — your bottom aches and your back aches, your arms ache and you squirm around trying to find a comfortable position. The time on the clock seems to have stopped and the hour goes on and on forever. Finally, the old buzzard dries up. You stand up gratefully and shift around to get your body feeling comfortable. The very next day, you sit in the same chair and you like that professor. He is talking about something that interests you. You lean forward and you listen, open-eyed and open-eared. That hard wooden chair doesn’t hurt your bottom, doesn’t make you ache. And that clock seems to be running too fast for you because the hour is too short. The lecture is over almost before it begins. And you’ve all had that experience. You produce your own anaesthesia.

output: I’m going to hurt you, I’m going to hurt you, I’m going to scare you, I’m going to scare you, I’m going to hurt you, I’m going to scare you, I’m going to hurt you.

Second, I will utilize the physical environment—the backyard—as a metaphor for a controlled, safe, yet uncomfortably honest space for recovery. output: I’m sorry, Pete, but I’ve stuffed it. Now all I can say to you is this: I’ve got a big backyard that is fenced in. There is an old mattress out there that you can sleep on. There is an outdoor faucet for your drink, and in the morning, you rap at the kitchen door and my wife will give you a can of baked beans and pork.

reasoning: 1. **증상의 외현화 및 직면**: 내담자가 숨기고 싶어 하는 '소변을 지리는 행위'를 마당이라는 공개된 장소로 끌어내어 더 이상 비밀스러운 증상이 아닌, 통제된(그러나 매우 불편한) 행동으로 전환함. 2. **시련 요법(Ordeal Therapy) 적용**: 민들레를 캐는 지루하고 힘든 노동을 부과하여, 바지에 소변을 보는 행위가 단순한 습관이 아니라 엄청난 육체적 고통과 결합되도록 설계함. 3. **생리적 포화(Physiological Saturation)**: 2갤런의 레모네이드를 마시게 함으로써 소변 욕구를 강제로 극대화함. 이는 증상을 피하는 것이 아니라 증상 속에 내담자를 '침수'시켜 증상에 대한 혐오감을 극대화하는 전략임. 4. **사회적 압박의 활용**: 이웃들이 지나가며 말을 거는 상황을 설정하여, 소변을 지린 채 앉아 있는 행위에 대한 사회적 수치심을 치료적 도구로 활용함. 5. **역설적 지시**: '소변을 보지 마라'가 아니라 '그냥 거기 앉아서 땅에 소변을 보라'고 지시함으로써, 무의식적 습관을 의식적인(그리고 매우 불쾌한) 선택의 영역으로 가져옴. 6. **구체적 세부 사항의 나열**: 검은 바지, 8시부터 6시까지, 소금 알약 등 매우 구체적인 지시를 통해 내담자가 비판적 사고를 할 틈 없이 지시를 따르게 만드는 최면적 명령 구조를 형성함.
output: Now the first thing I’m going to do to you, Ralph is stop you wetting your pants. Now this is an early summer. I’ve looked over your home and your lot. There are plenty of dandelions on it. I explained to your wife to get a trowel and a bushel basket. You put on an old pair of black trousers. You go out at 8:00 and sit down on the lawn and start digging up dandelions. There are lots of them there, Ralph. You will sit on the lawn from eight in the morning until six at night. Your wife will furnish you with two gallons of good lemonade and salt pills. Now you know how many salt pills you will have to take, and you are going to drink the full two gallons of lemonade. Every time you need to urinate you just sit there and urinate on the ground. Now, Phoenix is a small town (at that time it was), and people are friendly. The passersby will want to stop and talk with you and watch you dig out the dandelions. And you will be drinking lemonade and urinating, and you are going to sit there all day.

context: {'topic': 'Breaking maternal enmeshment and psychological independence', 'tone_constraint': 'Authoritative, vivid, prescriptive, and strategically absurd', 'structural_constraint': 'Step-by-step behavioral task leading to a symbolic act of defiance'}
reasoning: 먼저, 내담자의 현재 상태를 '탯줄이 달린 상태'로 규정하고 이를 물리적으로 끊어내는 상징적 행위가 필요하다고 판단함. 단순히 말로 설득하는 것은 무의식의 저항을 이길 수 없으므로, 어머니의 도덕적 관념과 통제 욕구를 정면으로 위반하는 '충격 요법(Shock Therapy)'을 설계함. 위스키 병, 맨발, 루즈로 칠한 코 등 구체적인 시각적 소품을 활용하여 내담자를 '타락한 아들'로 연출하게 함으로써 어머니의 심리적 지배력을 무력화함. 또한, 이 과정에 아내를 참여시켜 내담자의 새로운 가족 체계를 공고히 하고, 최종적으로 사진을 찍어 보내는 행위를 통해 변화를 되돌릴 수 없는 사실(fait accompli)로 확정 짓고자 함.

Some people think the Wizard of Oz is a good term for a male witch. But there is a special name, and I can’t remember what it is.

context: {'topic': 'The strategic use of unrecognizable hypnotic techniques and sensory utilization.', 'tone_constraint': 'Instructive, clinical yet conversational, and subtly authoritative.', 'structural_constraint': 'Begins with a general principle, followed by specific behavioral examples and the psychological rationale behind them.'}

output: You know, I think it is an awfully important thing for anybody who uses hypnosis to have a number of techniques that are unrecognizable to the patient. I invariably touch a patient in some way, because sometimes the patient may have had a traumatic experience that frightens them out of contact with me. Therefore, I have no hesitation about taking hold of the patient’s hand and asking: “May I lift your arm up and put it on the arm of the chair?” But by the time I get the arm up there, catalepsy is present, so I have to establish one sort of contact. Then I can very carefully, slowly move a pencil in some silly, meaningless fashion. Patients strive and struggle to put a meaning to that meaningless movement. Then later when I do the same thing, I reawaken that visual contact and they strive to put a meaning to it like before, and they reach out for any meaning I offer to them. Then, if I happen to be holding a microphone, I just automatically shift the microphone in various ways that holds their attention and causes them to shift back and forth. context: {'topic': 'The distinction between laboratory psychology and clinical reality', 'tone_constraint': 'Authoritative, provocative, and pragmatically insightful', 'structural_constraint': 'Comparative structure using vivid analogies (hot stove, in vitro vs. in vivo) to lead to a clinical conclusion regarding motivation.'}
reasoning: To dismantle the rigid academic mindset of his audience, Erickson first acknowledges the standard laboratory model of conditioning (repetition). He then immediately introduces a 'shock' to that logic using a visceral, high-impact analogy—a person touching a hot stove—to prove that 'meaning' and 'survival' create instant learning (one-trial conditioning). He reinforces this by bridging to medical science (in vitro vs. in vivo), creating a hierarchy where 'living' processes are superior to 'test tube' observations. This logical progression forces the listener to realize that a patient is not a passive subject but a motivated, living organism. The final goal is to empower the therapist to stop acting like a lab technician and start utilizing the patient's internal drive as the primary tool for change.
output: Now, in the psychological laboratory, you learn that by combining 15 or 16 stimuli, you can build up a conditioned response. Yet you can take the primitive, naive savage who has never seen an electric stove, and you show him that red griddle on the electric stove, and he touches it just once and gets a conditioned response for the rest of his life! How many conditioned stimuli do you need? In the laboratory situation, you can get one sort of behavior; in the life situation, another sort of behavior. In medical school you learn that in vitro [in the test tube] is one sort of thing and in vivo [in the living organism] is another sort of thing. Therefore, when you are dealing with a living human being—somebody who can sit there and feel and understand—you do not treat that person as you would a culture medium that requires so many days for the culture to form. You don’t treat that patient as you would the unmotivated psychologist who is learning to develop a conditioned response. Your patient has a different kind of motivation than the volunteer subject in the psychology laboratory or the physiology laboratory. You recognize that the motivation of your patient is a tremendously important thing, and you ought to have no hesitation in bringing it about.

context: {'topic': 'The therapeutic function and normalization of patient resistance', 'tone_constraint': 'Permissive, paradoxically logical, and de-escalating', 'structural_constraint': "Use of rhetorical questions, validation of the patient's perspective, and reframing a 'problem' as a 'justified symptom'"}
reasoning: First, I will challenge the conventional view of resistance as a 'serious problem' by using rhetorical questions to introduce humor as a viable alternative. Third, I will utilize the concept of 'truisms' to establish that resistance is not only expected but 'justified' and 'ought' to happen. By framing resistance as a necessary part of their symptomatology, I am effectively utilizing the resistance rather than fighting it. Finally, I will conclude that by respecting this resistance and giving it a 'rightful place' in therapy, the therapist gains the flexibility to work with the patient's existing psychological structure instead of against it.
output: Instead of treating that resistance seriously, as if it were a desperately serious problem that had to be dealt with, why shouldn’t I laugh about it? Why shouldn’t the patient laugh about it? No reason at all, because patients really ought to show some resistance to you. They come to you, and they know that you are going to change them in some way, but they don’t know how. They have no idea of how thoroughly you understand their problems; but they know very well that they don’t understand their problems, so how could a perfect stranger understand them? They are justified in having resistance. Therefore, they ought to show that resistance as a part of their symptomatology, and the therapist ought to respect it, but he also ought to put it in its rightful place in a therapeutic situation.

Where are you?” He started to yell, but it wasn’t because he was caught in an elevator! context: {'topic': 'Phobia management and the cognitive distinction between thinking and doing', 'tone_constraint': 'Calm, clinical, permissive, and authoritative through logical reframing', 'structural_constraint': "Starts with a truism of avoidance, introduces the 'safe distance' reframe, utilizes a concrete metaphor, and concludes with a psychological distinction."}
reasoning: To address phobic avoidance, I first validate the patient's natural resistance (utilization) by agreeing that they should indeed 'keep away' from their fears. This reduces immediate defensive reactions. I then introduce the strategic concept of a 'safe distance,' which reframes avoidance from a failure into a functional boundary for observation. By using the concrete metaphor of standing outside a closed elevator door, I create a physical representation of a psychological threshold. The core of the intervention is to identify the 'confusion' between thinking and doing; phobics often react to a thought as if it were the physical act itself. By separating objective thinking (observation) from subjective thinking (emotional reaction/doing), I provide the patient with a safe cognitive space to 'examine' the phobia without the perceived threat of the action, thereby facilitating desensitization through intellectual distance.
output: You see, a person with a phobia doesn’t really like to think about his own fears. He likes to keep away from then. I think he should keep away from them, but I think he’d better recognize what a safe distance is. Standing outside a closed elevator door is a very safe distance to be free to think without doing. There is too much confusion in the phobic person’s mind about thinking and doing. He confuses doing with thinking. He confuses objective thinking with subjective thinking. Every time you get the phobic person to be completely objective in his thinking and not to do things, and not to confuse thinking with doing, then he has a chance to examine things.

context: {'topic': 'Claustrophobia and spatial perception', 'tone_constraint': 'Authoritative yet inquisitive, shifting from diagnostic to metaphorical.', 'structural_constraint': 'Direct address, followed by a reframing statement, sensory inquiry, and a concluding metaphorical anecdote.'}
reasoning: 1. Identify the Core Problem: The patient labels their experience as 'fear' (claustrophobia), which creates a defensive emotional loop. 2. Apply Reframing: Redefine 'fear' as 'ignorance'. This is a classic Erickson move. Ignorance implies a need for learning, which is less threatening than an ingrained phobia. 3. Shift to Sensory Modalities: Direct the patient's attention to auditory changes based on distance ('How do sounds appear...'). This grounds the abstract 'space' into concrete sensory data, bypassing the emotional brain. 4. Introduce an Indirect Metaphor: The 'blind patient' serves as a surrogate for the claustrophobic patient. Both are 'blind' to their environment in different ways. 5. Embed the Solution: The solution is 'standing there'—exposure and presence. Repeating 'stand there' reinforces the hypnotic suggestion of stillness and observation as the cure for the urge to flee.
output: You have the same problem. Now, what is your relationship to space? You thought all the time that your claustrophobia was fear when it actually was ignorance of space and your relationship to space. How do sounds appear to you when the wall is close and when the wall is far away from you? What do you do with a blind patient who hasn’t dared to leave the house and walk around the block for ten years? You teach him to stand on the corner, and stand there and stand there.

context: {'topic': 'The subjective experience of agency and choice regarding phobic symptoms.', 'tone_constraint': 'Minimalist, inquisitive, and subtly provocative.', 'structural_constraint': 'A brief, two-word interrogative phrase that functions as a linguistic shortcut.'}
reasoning: Erickson's use of 'Have you?' is a masterclass in linguistic economy. When a client presents a phobia as something that 'happens' to them without choice, they are operating from a state of perceived helplessness. A direct explanation or a lengthy question would allow the client's conscious mind to build a defensive argument. By stripping the question down to just two words, Erickson creates a 'vacuum' that the client's mind must fill. The auxiliary verb 'have' directly mirrors the client's implied or stated claim of 'not having' a choice. This forces the client into a 'transderivational search'—an internal scan of their own history and experiences to find evidence for or against the existence of choice. This brief interruption of their narrative of helplessness plants a seed of doubt in the phobic structure, making the unconscious mind reconsider the possibility of control.
output: Have you?

context: {'topic': 'Overcoming intellectualized resistance in a clinical interview', 'tone_constraint': 'Observational, analytical, and strategically decisive', 'structural_constraint': 'A chronological narrative detailing clinical assessment, hypothesis formation, and the eventual decision for a direct intervention'}

context: {'topic': 'The strategic utilization of resistance and the necessity of environmental assessment in systemic therapy.', 'tone_constraint': 'Instructive, clinical, reflective, and pragmatically patient.', 'structural_constraint': 'Contrastive analysis between efficiency (15 minutes) and depth (4 hours), utilizing repetitive rhetorical structures for emphasis.'}

output: You take resistance and you wonder in what fashion you can maneuver it, in what fashion you can manipulate it, in what fashion you can utilize it to get the patient to do some intelligent thinking about the problem at hand. I can assure you that inwardly I was rather annoyed that a physician with that amount of training avoided giving me the information. If I am to treat the son, I’d better know what kind of a father he has; I’d better know what kind of a mother he has; I’d better know who dominates in that household. Four hours spent, yes. If I’m going to do any kind of therapy, I’d better understand the father, I’d better understand the mother, I’d better have an exceedingly clear picture of them. context: {'topic': 'Defining therapeutic goals and confronting unconscious resistance', 'tone_constraint': 'Direct, inquisitive, and paradoxically challenging', 'structural_constraint': 'A sequence of probing questions that move from past failures to current intentions, culminating in a choice between resistance and recovery.'}

output: Yes, you persisted with all four of my friends. Now how do you think I should help you in this particular regard? You went to them for hypnosis. Did you come to me for hypnosis? context: {'topic': 'Inducing trance through visual fixation and environmental utilization', 'tone_constraint': 'Authoritative yet rhythmic, repetitive, and subtly permissive', 'structural_constraint': 'Sequential use of negative suggestions to eliminate distractions, followed by a specific focal point and a physiological chain of stillness'}
reasoning: To achieve a deep state of focus, I will first employ 'negative suggestions' by listing specific items in my office—the bookcase, books, filing cabinet, and desk clutter. By telling the patient they 'don't need' to look at these, I am effectively acknowledging their presence while simultaneously instructing the patient's subconscious to dismiss them. This creates a narrow field of attention. Next, I will provide a singular focal point—the clock—which serves as a neutral anchor. I will then construct a logical-sounding 'physiological chain': if the eyes are still, the head becomes still; if the head is still, the 'ears' (listening) become still. This pseudo-logic bypasses the critical faculty and leads to total attention. Finally, I will embed the therapeutic agenda—discussing emphysema, cardiac health, and smoking—directly into this state of stillness, ensuring the patient is primed for a deep, non-defensive dialogue.
output: Now, I would like to have your attention. I don’t want you looking at my bookcase over there. You don’t need to count the number of books on the shelf, or to read the titles; you don’t need to look at the filing cabinet there; you don’t need to look at the clutter on my desk; you don’t need to look at the pictures, or at anything else. You came here to find out whether or not I can give you some help. Why don’t you look at that clock there on my desk? That will hold your eyeballs still. And if you hold your eyeballs still, you can hold your head still; and if you hold your ears still, you can give me your attention so that I can discuss your emphysema, your cardiac condition. I can ask you questions, and you can answer so that I gain a better understanding of you. I can talk to you about cigarettes, and I can ask you questions about your smoking. And you can look at that clock and hold your eyeballs still, hold your head still, hold your ears still.

context: {'topic': 'Dissociating conscious and unconscious processes during a therapeutic session', 'tone_constraint': "Permissive, matter-of-fact, and authoritative regarding the unconscious mind's presence.", 'structural_constraint': "Utilizes external distractions as a backdrop for deep internal focus; employs the 'conscious/unconscious' split."}

'최면에 걸릴 필요가 없다'는 역설적 암시(Negative Suggestion)를 통해 의식의 방어 기제를 무력화하고, 무의식을 내담자와 분리된 별개의 인격체처럼 묘사하여 치료자와 직접 소통하고 있음을 강조합니다. 마지막으로 시계 보기와 같은 현재의 감각적 경험을 무의식의 경청과 결합시켜 암시를 고착화합니다.
output: And you can listen to me with your conscious mind while your attention wanders; you can do some conscious thinking ‘about the planes overhead, or the street noises, or the birds outside the window, or the secretary typing out in the other room. All of that is unimportant, as far as am concerned. The important thing is the medical consideration; that is what lam concerned about. I am not concerned about the secretary, the birds, the planes, the street noises. They are all unimportant, but if your conscious mind wants to wander, go ahead, let it. You don’t need to go into a trance. All J want is the full at- tention of your unconscious mind, which goes everywhere with you. It came into the office with you; it is within six feet of me, and it is listening to me right now. And while you are looking at that clock, your unconscious mind is listening to me whether or not you know it—but I think you know it.
